input_text:
Voyager 1 embarked on its journey from Cape Canaveral on September 5, 1977. Although both probes were launched using a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket, Voyager 1's launch took place sixteen days after Voyager 2, despite the atypical paths assigned by NASA. The initial mission objective was to observe for five years, which included close approaches to Jupiter and Saturn. This alignment of the outer planets, occurring approximately every 176 years, presented a fortunate and infrequent opportunity.

Voyager 1 arrived at Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this encounter, the probe relayed the first high-definition images of Jupiter's faint rings and revealed active volcanoes on its moon Io, thereby documenting extraterrestrial volcanism for the first time. Two years later, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 approached Saturn at its closest point. It captured exceptionally clear data regarding Saturn’s ring system and secured detailed images of Titan. This trajectory around Saturn guided Voyager 1 beyond the plane of the solar system, concluding its planetary tour but setting it on a course toward interstellar space.

At the suggestion of astronomer Carl Sagan, on February 14, 1990, Voyager 1 oriented its instruments back toward Earth, capturing one photograph while it was roughly 6 billion kilometers distant. This solitary image, appearing as a single pixel against sunlight, earned the title Pale Blue Dot. This was the last picture transmitted by the spacecraft before the cameras were shut down to conserve power.

Voyager 1 officially entered interstellar space on August 25, 2012, upon crossing the boundary defined by the heliopause, making it the inaugural human-made object to reach that status. It carries the Golden Record, a 12-inch, gold-plated copper phonograph disc curated under Sagan’s guidance. This record contains greetings in 55 languages, alongside visuals and music intended for any future civilization that might encounter it. Its power source relies on three radioisotope thermoelectric generators powered by plutonium-238; the predictable decay of this isotope, with a half-life of 87.7 years, governs the available energy supply.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is situated about 25 billion kilometers away from Earth and maintains a velocity of approximately 61,000 kilometers per hour. In November 2026, it will achieve the milestone of covering a distance equivalent to one light-day.
output_text:
Voyager 1 began its mission from Cape Canaveral on September 5, 1977. Although both probes utilized a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket for launch, Voyager 1 launched sixteen days following Voyager 2, despite the unique paths prescribed by NASA. The primary goal of this mission was a five-year observation period, which included close passes by Jupiter and Saturn. The configuration of these outer planets, occurring roughly every 176 years, represented a rare and fortuitous alignment.

Voyager 1 reached Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this visit, the probe transmitted the first high-definition visuals of Jupiter’s faint rings and documented active volcanoes on its moon Io, marking the initial documentation of extraterrestrial volcanism. Two years later, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 made its closest approach to Saturn. It collected exceptionally sharp data concerning Saturn’s ring system and successfully imaged Titan. This trajectory around Saturn propelled Voyager 1 past the solar system’s plane, concluding its planetary tour and setting a course toward interstellar space.

At the suggestion of astronomer Carl Sagan, on February 14, 1990, Voyager 1 turned its instruments back toward Earth, sending one photograph while it was situated approximately 6 billion kilometers away. This singular image, appearing as a single pixel against sunlight, earned the title Pale Blue Dot. This transmission represented the last picture sent by the spacecraft before the cameras were powered down to conserve energy.

Voyager 1 formally entered interstellar space on August 25, 2012, having crossed the heliopause boundary, thereby becoming the first human-made object to achieve this status. It carries the Golden Record, a 12-inch, gold-plated copper phonograph disc curated following Sagan’s advice. This record contains greetings in 55 languages, along with visuals and music intended for any civilization that might eventually encounter it. Its power derives from three radioisotope thermoelectric generators fueled by plutonium-238; the consistent decay of this isotope, which has a half-life of 87.7 years, dictates the available power supply.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is positioned about 25 billion kilometers from Earth while maintaining a speed of around 61,000 kilometers per hour. In November 2026, it will reach the milestone of having traveled a distance equal to one light-day.
